name: 0-reduced method
statement_text:
A Runge–Kutta method (A, b , c) is ‘0-reducible’ if the
stage index set can be partitioned into two subsets {1, 2, . . . , s} = P0 ∪ P1
such that bi = 0 for all i ∈ P0 and such that aij = 0 if i ∈ P1 and j ∈ P0 .
The method formed by deleting all stages indexed by members of P0 is known
as the ‘0-reduced method’.
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
Consider a Runge--Kutta method with $s$ stages, coefficient matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{s\times s}$, weight vector $b\in\mathbb{R}^{s}$ and node vector $c\in\mathbb{R}^{s}$.  
Three equivalence relations are defined: equivalence, $\Phi$-equivalence and $P$-equivalence.  
$P$-equivalence involves reducibility, where a method can be simplified by eliminating or combining stages.  
$0$-reducibility formalizes one type of reducibility.

variables.name: s
variables.definition: number of stages in the Runge–Kutta method

preamble: The text defines three equivalence relations for Runge–Kutta methods: equivalence, Φ-equivalence, and P-equivalence. P-equivalence involves the concept of reducibility, where a method can be simplified by eliminating or combining stages. The definition of 0-reducibility formalizes one type of reducibility.
